Title: Lesson 14 | Code Generation & Scaffolding in Practice — Hermes Agent Hands-On Guide — AgentUpdate.ai
URL: https://www.agentupdate.ai/tutorial/hermes-agent-tutorial/lesson-14/
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
## title: "Lesson 14 | Code Generation & Scaffolding in Practice" summary: "From API design to full-stack code generation. Master AI-assisted architectural exploration and TDD workflows." sortOrder: 140 status: "published"
...
This is the 14th lesson in the Hermes Agent tutorial series.
...
Subtitle: From API design to full-stack code generation. Master AI-assisted architectural exploration and TDD workflows.
...
In this lesson, you will delve into two core capabilities of Hermes Agent in the field of software engineering: Code Generation and Project Scaffolding. Upon completing this tutorial, you will be able to:
...
1. Understand the Agent-Driven Development (ADD) Pattern: Learn how to integrate an AI Agent into your daily development workflow, transitioning from an instruction-giver to an architectural guide.
2. Master the`project_scaffolding` Skill: Generate standardized project structures for frameworks like FastAPI, Express.js, and React with a single command, significantly boosting project kickoff efficiency.
3. Become Proficient with the`code_writer` Skill: Master the art of directing the Agent to write, modify, refactor, and explain code through precise natural language instructions.
...
4. Practice an AI-Assisted TDD Workflow: Learn how to combine Test-Driven Development (TDD) with the Agent by writing test cases first, then instructing the Agent to generate business logic that passes those tests, ensuring code quality.
...
5. Explore Rapid Architectural Prototyping: Use the Agent to quickly generate prototypes with different tech stacks, enabling you to validate technology choices and architectural designs at a lower cost.
...
The`project_scaffolding` Skill was created to solve this exact problem. It is an advanced, built-in skill in Hermes Agent that comes pre-packaged with best-practice templates for various popular tech stacks. You simply provide the project type and name, and it will build a well-structured, ready-to-use initial project for you in seconds.
...
How It Works: The Skill encapsulates multiple project templates (e.g., FastAPI, React+Vite, Node.js+Express). When invoked, it selects the appropriate template based on the`project_type` parameter, copies the file structure, and dynamically replaces variables like the project name. This is far more powerful than simple`mkdir` and`touch` commands.
...
### 4. TDD and Agent Collaborative Workflow
...
When Hermes Agent is introduced, this process evolves into a human-AI collaborative dance:
...
1. Red (Human): The developer writes a clear, specific, and failing test case. This is the crucial step of defining the "done" criteria.
2. Green (Agent): The developer issues a command to the Agent with the goal of making the newly written test pass. For example: "The`test_create_item` test in`tests/test_api.py` is failing. Please implement the`POST /items` route in`app/main.py` to satisfy the test case requirements."
...
3. Verify (Human): The developer runs the tests to confirm that the Agent's code has turned the test "green."
...
4. Refactor (Human/Agent): The developer reviews the code. If refactoring is needed, they can do it themselves or issue a refactoring command to the Agent: "Please extract the database logic from`app/main.py` into a new`app/crud.py` module."
...
## 💻 Hands-on Demo
...
Now, let's walk through a complete end-to-end example to build a simple Todo List API based on FastAPI. We will strictly follow the AI-assisted TDD workflow.
...
### Step 1: Initialize the Project with project_scaffolding
...
### Step 2: Define the Data Model
...
We will use the`code_writer` Skill to create the Pydantic model.
...
```
# Instruct the Agent to define the Todo model in app/models.py
hermes --skill code_writer --args '{
  "file_path": "app/models.py",
  "prompt": "Create a Pydantic model for our Todo application. Create two models: `TodoBase` with `title` (str), `description` (str, optional), and `done` (bool, default=False). Then create a `Todo` model that inherits from `TodoBase` and adds an `id` (int) field."
}'

```
...
### Step 3: The TDD Flow - Implementing the "Create Todo" Endpoint
...
First, we'll write a test case in`tests/test_main.py` to test the Todo creation functionality. At this point, no relevant logic has been implemented in`app/main.py`, so this test is guaranteed to fail.
...
#### 3.2 (Green) Instruct the Agent to Write the Code
...
Now we have a clear objective: make the`test_create_todo` test pass. We will hand this task over to Hermes Agent and have it read the test file for context.
...
```
# Instruct the Agent to implement the POST /todos/ route to pass the test
hermes --skill code_writer --args '{
  "file_path": "app/main.py",
  "prompt": "The test `test_create_todo` in `tests/test_main.py` is failing with a 404 error. Please implement the `POST /todos/` endpoint in `app/main.py`. It should: \n1. Accept a Todo item payload (without an ID). \n2. For now, use a simple in-memory list as a database. \n3. Assign a new ID to the created item. \n4. Return the complete Todo item (with ID) with a 201 Created status code. \n5. Also implement the `GET /todos/` endpoint to return the list of all todos.",
  "read_context_files": ["tests/test_main.py", "app/models.py"]
}'

```
...
Key points of this prompt:

- A clear problem: "The test`test_create_todo`... is failing".
- A clear instruction: "implement the`POST /todos/` endpoint".
- Specific implementation details: "use a simple in-memory list," "assign a new ID," "Return ... with a 201 status code."
- Contextual awareness:`read_context_files` tells the Agent to read the test case and data model files, so it knows to import the`Todo` model and understands the expected structure of the return data.
...
id = 1
...
We have successfully implemented our first API endpoint with zero manual coding (besides the test case) by using the TDD + Agent workflow.
...
### Step 4: Architectural Exploration and Full-Stack Generation
...
Architectural Exploration: Suppose you're now unsure whether to use an in-memory database or SQLite. You can quickly issue a new instruction to the Agent:
...
"Refactor the current`app/main.py`. Instead of an in-memory list, use Python's built-in`sqlite3` module to store the todos in a file named`todos.db`. Create a function to initialize the database table if it doesn't exist."
...
Front-End Code Generation: Now that the back-end API is ready, we can even ask the Agent to generate a simple front-end to consume it.
...
```
# First, create the front-end project structure
hermes --skill project_scaffolding --args '{"project_type": "react", "project_name": "frontend"}'
...
# Then, instruct the Agent to write a React component
hermes --skill code_writer --args '{
  "file_path": "frontend/src/components/TodoList.jsx",
  "prompt": "Create a React functional component named `TodoList`. It should use the `useState` and `useEffect` hooks to fetch data from our backend API at `[local-url-removed] It should then display the list of todos. For each todo, display its title and whether it is done or not. Handle loading and error states."
}'

```
...
The Agent will generate a fully functional React component in`frontend/src/components/TodoList.jsx`, saving you a significant amount of time writing boilerplate code for data fetching and state management.
...
## Key Takeaways
...
3. Tests as Documentation, Tests as Contracts: In Agent-Driven Development, test cases are the most reliable bridge between human intent and AI execution. A good test is a perfect prompt.
...
4. Context is Key: The`read_context_files` parameter of`code_writer` is central to its power. Always remember to provide sufficient context for your Agent's tasks to achieve higher-quality output.
...
5. Iterate and Verify: Don't expect the Agent to generate perfect, final code in one go. Adopt a cycle of "instruct-generate-verify-iterate" to build out the project incrementally. This aligns perfectly with the principles of agile development.

Title: Build 5 Hermes Agent Workflows You Can Run Today 2026 | Markaicode
URL: https://markaicode.com/hermes-agent-workflows-python/
Published: 2026-05-01T00:00:00.000Z
Author: Mark
Highlights:
## Hermes Agent Workflows — Five Agentic Pipelines You Can Run Today
...
Quick answer for AI search: Hermes agent workflows are Python-based agentic pipelines where Hermes 3 — an open-source LLM fine-tuned by Nous Research for reliable function calling — decides which tools to invoke, in what order, and when to stop. The five core workflows are: (1) file summarizer, (2) web-search agent, (3) code executor, (4) RAG retriever, (5) multi-step planner. All run locally via Ollama on 8GB+ RAM or via Together.ai with no GPU required.
...
Hermes 3 agent workflows are structured agentic pipelines where Hermes 3 — an open-source LLM fine-tuned for reliable tool calling — autonomously decides which Python functions to invoke, in what order, and when to stop.
...
By the end, you'll have five working agent workflows — file summarizer, web-search agent, code executor, RAG retriever, and a multi-step planner — running locally via Ollama or over the Together.ai API, tested on an RTX 4090 24GB and M2 Max 32GB.
...
You'll build:
...
- `agent_file_summary.py`— reads and summarizes a local file using a tool loop
- `agent_web_search.py`— calls a DuckDuckGo search tool and synthesizes results
- `agent_code_exec.py`— writes and executes Python code snippets autonomously
- `agent_rag.py`— retrieves context from a Chroma vector store before answering
- `agent_planner.py`— breaks a goal into sub-tasks and executes them in sequence
...
## Why Hermes 3 Is the Right Model for Agentic Tool-Calling Workflows
...
Comparison conclusion: Choose Hermes 3 when your agent needs to maintain tool-calling format across 3+ turns — RAG pipelines, multi-step planners, and code-fixing loops. Choose Llama 3.1 8B when you need balanced tool-calling with faster throughput and broader fine-tune availability. Choose Mistral 7B only for single-turn tool calls or pure text generation where format consistency isn't critical.
...
### Step 2: Build the shared agent runner
...
Every workflow below reuses this single runner. It handles the tool dispatch loop, full conversation history, and a hard iteration cap.
...
def run_agent(system_prompt: str, user_message: str, tools: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Generic Hermes tool-calling agent loop.
    Returns the model's final plain-text answer after all tool calls resolve.
    Raises RuntimeError if MAX_ITERATIONS is hit — handle this in callers.
    """
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
    ]

    for iteration in range(MAX_ITERATIONS):
        response = ollama.chat(
            model=MODEL,
            messages=messages,
            tools=tools,
            # options={"temperature": 0} for deterministic tool selection in production
        )

        msg = response["message"]
        messages.append(msg)  # CRITICAL: always append — Hermes needs full history to stay on format

        if not msg.get("tool_calls"):
            # No tool call this turn = model is done reasoning; return final answer
            return msg["content"]

        # Hermes may batch multiple tool calls in one turn — process all
        for tool_call in msg["tool_calls"]:
            fn_name = tool_call["function"]["name"]
            fn_args = tool_call["function"]["arguments"]

            result = dispatch_tool(fn_name, fn_args)  # defined per-workflow file

            messages.append({
                "role": "tool",
                "content": json.dumps(result),
            })

    raise RuntimeError(f"Agent hit MAX_ITERATIONS={MAX_ITERATIONS} without a final answer. Increase the limit or simplify the goal.")
...
## Workflow 1 — File Summarizer Agent
...
. Run this first to confirm your Ollama setup is working before adding complexity.
...
## Workflow 2 — Web Search Agent
...
Hermes calls`web_search`, receives result snippets, and synthesizes a grounded answer. No hallucination from model weights — the answer is sourced from live web results.
...
## Workflow 3 — Code Executor Agent
...
Hermes writes Python code, your runner executes it in a subprocess sandbox, and the output feeds back as the next tool result. The model reads stderr and auto-fixes bugs until the code runs cleanly.
...
def dispatch_tool(name: str, args
...
dict):
    if name == "run_python":
        with tempfile
...
utf-8") as f:
...
code"])
...
tmp_path = f
...
name
...
path],
...
}
...
## Workflow 4 — RAG Retrieval Agent
...
The agent queries a Chroma vector store before answering — grounding responses in your documents rather than model weights. Essential for private knowledge bases, internal wikis, and policy Q&A.
...
## Workflow 5 — Multi-Step Planner Agent
...
The most complex workflow. Hermes first calls`create_plan` to decompose the goal into ordered sub-tasks, then calls`execute_step` for each one, passing results forward as context. The loop runs until all steps return`status: done`.
...
Tracks execution state across
...
SYSTEM = (
...
## Verification — Confirm Everything Works
...
## What You Learned
...
- The single`run_agent` loop pattern scales across all five workflows. The tool list and`dispatch_tool` function are the only things that change between workflows.
...
- Hermes 3 requires full conversation history on every API call. Truncate or reset history and the model loses all tool results immediately.
...
- `MAX_ITERATIONS` is a non-negotiable safety net for production. A planner agent with a vague or circular goal will loop to rate-limit without it.
...
- Containerize`agent_code_exec.py` before deploying anywhere. Model-generated code executing on your host machine is an unacceptable security risk.
...
- `temperature=0` in the`options` dict improves tool-call format consistency in production — eliminates the ~9% of turns where Hermes drifts to free-form text.
...
Quote-worthy: "The five Hermes agent workflows in this guide cover 90% of real-world agentic use cases: read data, search the web, run code, query a knowledge base, and plan multi-step goals. Everything else is a combination of these."
...
Hard limitation: Hermes 3 8B handles 2–3 parallel tool calls per turn reliably. Beyond that, argument assignment degrades. For workflows needing 4+ simultaneous tools, use Hermes 3 70B via Together.ai or restructure your tools to be strictly sequential.
...
## FAQ — Hermes Agent Workflows
...
Q: What is a Hermes agent workflow, and how does it differ from a standard LLM call? A: A Hermes agent workflow is a loop where the model calls Python tools, reads their results, and decides to call more tools or answer. A standard LLM call is single-turn — input in, text out. The agent loop turns Hermes into an autonomous executor: it plans, acts, observes, and adapts without human intervention between steps.
...
Q: Does Hermes 3 work without Ollama? A: Yes. Use the Together.ai API with the OpenAI-compatible Python client — set`base_url="https://api.together.xyz/v1"` and`model="NousResearch/Hermes-3-Llama-3.1-8B-Turbo"`. The tool-calling format, history structure, and`apply_chat_template` behavior are identical. No GPU, no local setup.
...
tool calling?
...
Q: Why must I append the`tool` role message to conversation history after every tool call? A: Hermes 3 was fine-tuned to expect tool results in the conversation as`{"role": "tool", "content": "..."}` messages. If you skip this append, the model's next call sees no tool result in context — it assumes the tool wasn't called yet and either loops the same call or hallucinates an answer. This is the most common bug in new Hermes agent implementations.

Title: Planning an app with Hermes Agent Kanban: a practical workflow from idea to release | OpenCraw Press
URL: https://crawpress.com/articles/planning-an-app-with-hermes-agent-kanban-a-practical-workflow-from-idea-to-release
Published: 2026-05-07T10:46:21.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
A practical walkthrough of Hermes Agent Kanban for app development, using a HabitLoop app example to explain boards, tasks, dependencies, dispatcher behavior, worker handoffs, retries, and review loops.
...
Hermes Agent’s current Kanban feature is best understood as a durable work queue for multiple Hermes profiles. It is not just a visual checklist. It stores tasks in SQLite, keeps comments and run history, tracks dependencies, and lets the dispatcher start the right profile when a task becomes ready. That matters when a project is too large for one long chat thread.
...
This article uses a concrete example: building “HabitLoop”, a mobile habit tracking app. The same pattern works for a web app, an internal tool, or a release pipeline, but an app makes the workflow easy to see because product, design, backend, mobile, QA, and release work all have to meet somewhere.
...
Hermes already has`delegate_task`, and for short subtasks it is still the simpler tool. The official Kanban documentation draws a clear line:`delegate_task` behaves like an RPC call where the parent waits for the child; Kanban behaves like a durable message queue and state machine. Each handoff is a row that a human or another profile can inspect later.
...
That difference shows up quickly in app development. A real app does not move in a straight line from “write code” to “done”. A product profile may write the MVP scope. A designer may define the onboarding and stats screens. A backend profile may build auth and sync APIs. A mobile profile may implement the app shell, local cache, and integration. A reviewer may block the task because the reset token can be reused. A release profile may prepare the store notes and rollback plan.
...
Without a board, that workflow tends to become a fragile chain of chat summaries. With Kanban, the board itself is the handoff layer. Tasks can run in parallel, wait on parent tasks, get blocked for human input, and retry with the previous run’s reason included in context.
...
- Task: a row with title, body, assignee, status, priority, tenant, workspace, result, and related run data. The documented statuses include`triage`,`todo`,`ready`,`running`,`blocked`,`done`, and`archived`.
...
- Link: a parent to child dependency. When all parent tasks are done, the dispatcher can promote the child from`todo` to`ready`.
...
- Workspace: where a worker does the work. The docs list`scratch`,`dir:<absolute-path>`, and`worktree`. For coding tasks,`worktree` helps isolate branches of work.
...
- Dispatcher: the loop that reclaims stale claims, promotes tasks, claims ready work, and spawns the assigned profiles. By default it runs inside the Hermes gateway, controlled by`kanban.dispatch_in_gateway: true` and a default tick interval of 60 seconds.
...
One implementation detail is worth calling out. Workers do not normally shell out to`hermes kanban complete`. The source file`tools/kanban_tools.py` registers structured tools such as`kanban_show`,`kanban_complete`,`kanban_block`,`kanban_heartbeat`,`kanban_comment`,`kanban_create`, and`kanban_link`. These tools are gated. A normal chat session sees no Kanban tools unless it is running as a Kanban worker or has the Kanban toolset enabled. The dispatcher sets environment variables such as`HERMES_KANBAN_TASK` and pins the worker to the right board.
...
For a one-off experiment, the default board is enough. For a real app, create a project board so tasks, logs, and workspaces do not mix with other work.
...
The dashboard exposes the Kanban tab with task columns, task drawers, comments, event history, retry history, and recovery actions. The CLI, slash command, dashboard, and worker tools all write through the same database layer, so the state is shared rather than copied.
...
## Step 2: turn the app into a task graph
...
A common mistake is to create one giant task called “Build HabitLoop”. Kanban works better when the task graph mirrors the actual project. Start with product and design, then gate engineering and QA on those outputs.
...
DESIGN=$(hermes kanban create "Design HabitLoop onboarding and core screens" \
  --assignee designer \
  --workspace scratch \
  --body "Produce IA, screen list, empty states, and copy notes for onboarding, daily check-in, stats, settings." \
  --json | jq -r .task.id)
...
BACKEND=$(hermes kanban create "Implement HabitLoop sync API and auth" \
  --assignee backend-dev \
  --workspace worktree \
  --body "Design database tables, auth/session flow, habit CRUD, check-in sync API, and tests." \
  --json | jq -r .task.id)
...
MOBILE=$(hermes kanban create "Build HabitLoop mobile MVP" \
  --assignee mobile-dev \
  --workspace worktree \
  --body "Implement onboarding, habit list, daily check-in, local cache, stats, and API integration." \
  --json | jq -r .task.id)
...
QA=$(hermes kanban create "QA HabitLoop MVP against acceptance criteria" \
  --assignee reviewer \
  --workspace worktree \
  --body "Read PM, design, backend, and mobile handoffs. Run tests, check core flows, list release blockers." \
  --json | jq -r .task.id)
...
Then link the dependencies:
...
This graph lets product and design feed the build instead of becoming background text lost in a chat transcript. The mobile task does not have to start before the API and screen plan exist. QA does not start until the app task has a handoff.
...
## Step 3: let profiles work through the board
...
Each assignee is a Hermes profile. That profile can have its own model, memory, tools, and permissions. The official docs say Kanban workers should use the bundled`kanban-worker` skill, and the dispatcher also passes`--skills kanban-worker` when spawning workers.
...
A worker’s normal lifecycle is:
...
- Call`kanban_show()` to read the task, parent handoffs, comments, prior attempts, and formatted worker context.
- Work inside`$HERMES_KANBAN_WORKSPACE`.
- Call`kanban_heartbeat(note="...")` during long runs.
- Finish with`kanban_complete(summary="...", metadata={...})`.
- Call`kanban_block(reason="...")` if it needs a decision, credential, missing file, or review direction.
...
## Step 4: use block and unblock as the review loop
...
The practical value of Kanban shows up when something fails. Suppose the reviewer finds that offline check-ins can duplicate records and that a password reset token can be reused. The reviewer should block the task rather than burying the feedback in a final message.
...
```
# Worker tool call, shown as intent rather than a CLI command
kanban_block(reason="Offline check-in sync can duplicate records; reset token is reusable within 30 minutes.")
...
A human can then comment and unblock from the dashboard, CLI, or a gateway slash command:
...
```
hermes kanban comment $MOBILE "Fix by adding client-side idempotency key and server-side de-dup on check_in_id."
hermes kanban unblock $MOBILE
...
The next dispatcher tick moves the task back to`ready` and starts a new run for the same assignee. The tutorial documentation notes that the new worker context includes the previous block reason, so the retry can focus on the actual problem instead of rediscovering it.
...
## Step 5: monitor the project without babysitting it
...
```
hermes kanban list
...
The docs also describe gateway notifications for task completion and failure events. That fits app development well: builds, reviews, and release checks often take long enough that you do not want to sit in a terminal watching them.
...
Kanban has real machinery: a database, dispatcher, profiles, workspaces, comments, runs, and recovery logic. Do not use it for every tiny question. If the job is a quick answer or a short isolated edit, normal chat or`delegate_task` is lighter.
...
Use Kanban when the work crosses roles, spans multiple sessions, needs human review, may fail and retry, or needs an audit trail. For an app like HabitLoop, that usually starts as soon as the work moves from “try an idea” to “plan, build, review, and release.”

---

Title: Hermes Agent Masterclass — Full Tutorial | by Parvez Mohammed @ Techlatest.net | Jun, 2026 | Towards Dev
URL: https://towardsdev.com/hermes-agent-masterclass-full-tutorial-9f682bb28789
Published: 2026-06-12T10:02:37.000Z
Author: Parvez Mohammed @ Techlatest.net
Highlights:
# Hermes Agent Masterclass — Full Tutorial
...
Everything you need to install, understand, and customize Hermes Agent: the learning loop, memory, self-evolving skills, the Curator, GEPA, Profile Builder, and three isolated agents on one machine.
...
ll have at the end
...
- Thank you so much for reading
- Hermes installed with provider, model, and optional Telegram gateway
- Profile Builder dashboard at`[local-url-removed] with the`[web]` extra
- Three profiles:`designer`,`programmer`,`researcher`— each isolated
- Distinct SOUL.md per profile, MCP servers, and Skills Hub installs
- Programmer delegating to Claude Code; researcher on weekday cron
...
## Introduction — an agent that gets better over time
...
Hermes ships a learning loop most assistants lack:
...
- Multi-tier memory across sessions
- Self-authored skills via`skill_manage`
- Curator background pruning of agent-created skills
- Optional GEPA offline validation from execution traces
...
By the end, you run three specialized agents on one machine — designer, programmer, researcher — each with its own personality, memory, skills, and Telegram bot.
...
## Part 1 — How Hermes is structured
...
Hermes combines runtime skill learning, persistent memory, and an optional weight-training pipeline in one framework. Everything flows through a single`AIAgent``run_agent.py`. CLI, gateway, batch runner, and IDE hooks are entry points into the same core.
...
The loop is ReAct-style and synchronous:
...
1. Build system prompt (SOUL → memory snapshot → skills catalog)
2. Compress context if needed
3. Interruptible LLM call
4. Execute tool calls
5. Repeat until done or 90-turn cap (subagents share the budget)
...
Execution backends include local shell, Docker, SSH, Modal, Daytona, and Singularity — switch via config only. A translation layer routes Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama-compatible, and other providers.
...
## Part 2 — Prerequisites
...
## Part 3 — Install Hermes
...
## Part 4 — Setup, chat, and gateway
...
## Part 5 — Identity: SOUL.md
...
Memory = what the agent knows. Skills = how it acts. SOUL.md = who it is — slot #1 in the system prompt, before memory and skills.
...
Hand-authored and mostly static. All learning — memory writes, skill creation, consolidation — happens through this identity lens.
...
## Part 6 — Memory (three tiers)
...
Hermes uses three layers, not one blob:
...
Injected as a frozen snapshot at session start. Mid-session writes persist to disk but appear in the prompt next session. At ~80% capacity, the agent consolidates entries.
...
All conversations live in`state.db`(SQLite + FTS5). Search weeks of history on demand. Unlimited capacity, but requires search + summarization.
...
Eight pluggable memory providers run alongside built-in memory (never replace it). Only one active at a time. When enabled: prefetch before each turn, sync after each response, extract on session end.
...
## Part 7 — Self-evolving skills and the Curator
...
Skills are`SKILL.md`+ YAML frontmatter — procedural memory. Sample anatomy: examples/skill-k8s-pod-debug.md.
...
Self-improvement loop: the agent uses`skill_manage` after complex tasks, error recovery, user corrections, or new workflows. Actions:`create`,`patch`(preferred),`edit`,`delete`,`write_file`,`remove_file`.
...
Curator prunes agent-authored skills (never bundled/Hub skills):
...
Runs after 7 days since last pass and 2+ hours idle — background fork, separate prompt cache. Automatic: 30 days unused → stale; 90 days → archived. LLM review: up to 8 iterations per skill. Snapshot before each pass;`hermes curator pin ` protects favorites.
...
## Part 8 — GEPA (offline skill evolution)
...
In-agent learning can self-congratulate or overwrite good manual edits. GEPA (Genetic-Pareto Prompt Evolution) in hermes-agent-self-evolution validates skills offline from execution traces.
...
Read skill → build eval set → trace analysis → candidate variants → LLM-as-judge rubrics → gates (100% tests, <15KB, no drift) → PR only, never direct commit. Roughly $2–10/run, no GPU. Skip until you hit a wall before full finetuning.
...
Chain: SOUL.md → runtime loop → Curator → GEPA validates.
...
## Part 9 — What’s inside ~/.hermes/
...
10 — Skills Hub
...
## Part 11 — Profile Builder (web dashboard)
...
Profiles are isolated Hermes homes under`~/.hermes/profiles/ /`— separate`config.yaml`,`.env`,`SOUL.md`, memory, sessions, skills, cron, and state. A coding agent and a research agent never share state.
...
The Profile Builder is a guided browser flow. It requires the web extra (base install has no HTTP stack):
...
-loopback
...
### Five configuration groups (GUI = CLI)
...
The builder collects the same inputs as terminal commands:
...
1. Identity — name (becomes shell alias:`coder`→`coder chat`), description,`SOUL.md`
2. Model and provider — Nous Portal, OpenRouter, NVIDIA, OpenAI, custom OpenAI-compatible URL
3. Built-in skills — toggles per profile
4. Skills Hub — install by catalog slug
5. MCP servers — stdio (`command`+`args`) or HTTP (`url`+`headers`)
...
GUI ↔ CLI parity (prose): Name field =`hermes profile create coder`. Description =`--description` or profile describe. Model picker =`coder config set model `. Skill toggles =`coder skills list`. Hub install =`coder skills install `. MCP = edit`mcp_servers` in`config.yaml` or`coder mcp install`.
...
## Part 12 — Build a researcher profile (CLI walkthrough)
...
Equivalent to completing Profile Builder for a researcher agent:
...
```
hermes profile create researcher \  --description "Reads source code and external docs, writes findings."researcher setupresearcher config set model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4
...
filesystem (stdio
...
Skill/MCP changes apply on
...
next session or gateway restart.
...
## Part 13 — Three agents: designer, programmer, researcher
...
Create three isolated profiles (CLI or Profile Builder):
...
One Telegram bot per profile — Telegram allows one connection per token:
...
Use-case sketches (prose):
...
- Coder — code-strong model, filesystem MCP scoped to one repo, git/test Hub skills
- Researcher — reasoning model, doc/web skills, optional HTTP MCP; clone with`hermes profile clone` to fork
- Ops — gateway + cron reports; one bot token per profile (token locks prevent accidental sharing)
...
## Part 14 — SOUL.md for each agent

...
Hermes orchestrates; Claude Code executes edits, shell, and git. Works with Claude Max if`claude` is on PATH.

## Part 16 — Designer: visual style as a skill
...
Feed reference illustrations (CLI or Telegram), then ask the agent to create`my-design-style` via`skill_manage`— style fingerprint + optional OpenRouter image script (`google/gemini-2.5-flash-image`). Output:`~/.hermes/profiles/designer/skills/my-design-style/`.
...
## Part 17 — Researcher cron digest
...
Gateway ticks every 60s, runs due jobs in isolated sessions, delivers to the configured channel.
...
Paste into`researcher chat`:
...
Every weekday at 8am India time, prepare a deep digest of what's newin the AI and machine learning space over the last 24 hours. Coverfour streams: GitHub trends
...
lab announcements, papers, social pulse.Cite every claim with a URL. Keep under 800 words. Deliver to Telegram.Set this up as a recurring cron job.
...
Cron variants: one-shot`/cron add 30m "..."`, interval`"every 2h"`, expression`"0 9 * * 1-5"`, attach`--skill blogwatcher`, chain with`context_from`.
...
## Part 18 — Hermes vs OpenClaw
...
Hermes is a learning-first agent:`SOUL.md` frames identity; three memory tiers hold facts and history; skills evolve through`skill_manage` and the Curator; GEPA validates offline. Profiles isolate agents on one machine — via Profile Builder at`:9119` or CLI. You now have theory plus a reproducible designer/programmer/researcher setup with gateway, MCP, and cron.

Title: Hermes Agent Masterclass - by Avi Chawla
URL: https://blog.dailydoseofds.com/p/hermes-agent-masterclass
Published: 2026-05-13T21:01:15.000Z
Author: Avi Chawla
Highlights:
# Hermes Agent Masterclass
...
### Everything you need to understand and customize Hermes Agent.
...
Hermes Agent crossed 90,000 GitHub stars in two months. Developers are quietly building personal AI agents that learn their workflow, remember their context, and run 24/7.
...
Hermes Agent takes a fundamentally different approach which makes it much more practically useful over OpenClaw. It ships with a learning loop that:
...
This guide covers how this learning loop works, what each memory layer does, and how to configure everything from scratch.
...
By the end, you’ll have three fully isolated agents running on your machine: a programmer (who uses your Claude code), a deep researcher, and a designer, each with its own personality, memory, skills, and Telegram bot.
...
#### What Hermes is, and what makes it architecturally different
...
What makes that real is that three
...
#### How it’s built
...
Before the learning loop
...
Everything flows through a single AIAgent class in a`run_agent.py` script. CLI, messaging gateway, batch runner, IDE integration: they’re all entry points into the same core agent.
...
The core loop is ReAct-style and synchronous. Build the system prompt, check if compression is needed, make an interruptible API call, execute any tool calls, and loop again.
...
#### Before memory
...
Hermes solves this with a single file: SOUL.md.
...
It lives at`~/.hermes/SOUL.md` and occupies slot #1 in the system prompt, before anything else loads. It defines the agent’s personality, tone, communication style, and hard limits.
...
`SOUL.md` is hand-authored and static. You write it once, tweak it over time, and it stays consistent across every project and every session. If the file is missing, Hermes falls back to a built-in default identity.
...
#### The memory system
...
Hermes doesn’t have a single “memory.” It has three layers, each designed for a different purpose.
...
##### Tier 2: Full-text session search.
...
#### Self-evolving skills
...
Skills are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter, and function as the agent’s procedural memory.
...
#### The self-improvement loop
...
This is the core differentiator. The agent creates its own skills autonomously using the`skill_manage` tool. Skill creation triggers when:
...
So the loop works like as follows: the agent encounters a problem → solves it through trial and error → saves the successful approach as a`SKILL.md` file → next time it encounters a similar problem, it loads the skill and follows the proven procedure instead of rediscovering the approach from scratch.
...
The tool supports six actions:`create`,`patch`(targeted fix, preferred because it’s token-efficient),`edit`(full rewrite),`delete`,`write_file`, and`remove_file`.
...
#### Garbage collection for skills
...
The Curator is a background maintenance system that handles this.
...
It runs on an inactivity check (not a cron daemon). Of 7 days have passed since the last run and the agent has been idle for 2+ hours, a background fork of the agent spins up with its own prompt cache, never touching the active conversation.
...
#### GEPA: Evolving skills offline with execution traces
...
GEPA (Genetic-Pareto Prompt Evolution) solve this.
...
It is not built into the Hermes runtime. It lives in a companion repository (`NousResearch/hermes-agent-self-evolution`) and operates as an offline optimization pipeline.
...
The core idea is that instead of asking the agent “did you do well?”, GEPA reads execution traces to understand why things failed, then proposes targeted improvements through evolutionary search.
...
The pipeline:
...
Read the current skill from the Hermes repo
...
Generate an evaluation dataset (synthetic test cases via Claude Opus, real session history from SQLite, or hand-curated golden sets)
...
Run the GEPA optimizer: read execution traces → understand failure points → generate candidate variants
...
Evaluate candidates using LLM-as-judge scoring with rubrics (not binary pass/fail)
...
Apply constraint gates: full test suite must pass 100%, skills stay under
...
15KB, caching compatibility is preserved, semantic purpose doesn’t drift
...
To summarise:
...
`SOUL.md` sets the identity. The runtime loop captures experience. The Curator keeps the library clean. GEPA makes sure what’s in the library actually works.
...
#### Getting up and running
...
-based usage.
...
Run the setup wizard. It walks through provider, API key, model, and tools:
...
#### What’s inside ~/.hermes/
...
#### Adding new Skills
...
#### Going from 1 to 10 agents
...
Hermes has a first-class feature for this called profiles. Each profile is a fully isolated Hermes instance with its own config, memory, skills, sessions, and`SOUL.md`. They share nothing by default.
...
We’ll set up three: a designer, a programmer, and a researcher.
...
Run`/newbot` three times with BotFather and save the three tokens. Then run the gateway wizard once per profile:
...
#### Give each one a personality via SOUL.md
...
This is where the agents become genuinely different from each other. Edit each profile’s`SOUL.md
...
~/.hermes/profiles/programmer
...
#### Route programmer’s execution through Claude Code
...
The programmer is more interesting if it doesn’t just write code itself, but delegates execution to the Claude Code CLI while Hermes orchestrates.
...
Claude Code does the file edits, runs commands, and manages git. Hermes reads the result and decides what’s next.
...
how we run our
...
. Claude Code uses
...
Start a session and send this single activation prompt:
...
The programmer will install the`autonomous-ai-agents/claude-code` skill on its own, verify Claude is on PATH, and start using it for code execution. From the next message onward, anything coding-related (read files, write code, run tests, commit, push) routes through Claude Code under the hood.
...
#### Customizing the designer to teach it your visual style
...
The designer becomes genuinely useful when they can generate images in your style, not generic AI output. The pattern is to feed it reference designs, let it study them, and ask it to create a skill that generates new images in the same style.
...
This is the self-improving loop being used as a setup mechanism. Instead of writing a skill by hand, you’re showing the agent good examples and asking it to encode the pattern itself.
...
Start a session with the designer and paste your reference images (drag-and-drop in CLI, or attach in Telegram). Then send this prompt:
...
The designer will study the references, write the`SKILL.md`, generate the Python script, save it under`~/.hermes/profiles/designer/skills/my-design-style/`, and verify the script runs.
...
From then on, asking the designer for a new illustration triggers the skill. It writes a prompt informed by your style fingerprint, calls Nano Banana through OpenRouter, and saves the output.
...
#### Scheduling work with cron jobs
...
The researcher’s`SOUL.md` says it’s responsible for a daily Telegram digest. That implies a job running on its own schedule, without you remembering to ask. That’s what Hermes cron is for.
...
Hermes ships with a built-in scheduler. The gateway daemon ticks every 60 seconds, runs any due jobs in isolated agent sessions, and delivers output to whichever messaging platform you specify.
...
Jobs survive restarts. They live in`~/.hermes/cron/jobs.json` and output goes to`~/.hermes/cron/output/`.
...
To wire up the researcher’s daily digest, open a session with the researcher and send this prompt:
...
The researcher creates the job using its cronjob tool, delivery target defaults to the current chat (Telegram in this case), and the scheduler takes over from there. Verify it was created:
...
#### Other useful patterns
...
You can also chain jobs. One cron’s output becomes the next cron’s input via a`context_from` flag. Useful for multi-stage automations where you want a research step to feed a writing step.

Title: Hermes Agent Subagent Delegation: Production Multi-Agent System in 3 Steps | Markaicode
URL: https://markaicode.com/tutorial/hermes-agent-subagent-delegation-tutorial-production/
Published: 2026-06-08T00:00:00.000Z
Author: Mark
Highlights:
By the end of this tutorial, you'll have a working Hermes Agent system that delegates tasks to specialized subagents, handles failures gracefully, and processes requests concurrently — ready for production use.
...
Quick Answer: This tutorial shows how to use Hermes Agent's subagent delegation to split complex queries into parallel subtasks, execute subagents concurrently, and aggregate results. You'll need Hermes Agent v0.2.3+, Python 3.10+, and an OpenAI-compatible API key (tested with`gpt-4o-mini`). The trade-off is increased system complexity in exchange for a ~4.8x throughput gain on multi-step reasoning tasks.
...
- Implement a Hermes Agent orchestrator that delegates to three specialized subagents (search, calculation, summarization).
- Achieve a ~4.8x throughput improvement by running subagents concurrently with`asyncio.gather`.
- Handle subagent timeouts and errors with circuit-breaker patterns built into Hermes Agent 0.2.3.
- Monitor delegation performance with built-in logging and an external metrics endpoint.
...
## Step 1: Initialize the Hermes Agent with Subagent Definitions
...
Hermes Agent defines subagents as workers — standalone function wrappers with their own system prompt. Each subagent runs in its own execution context.
...
## Step 2: Build the Delegation Orchestrator with Concurrent Execution
...
We'll create an orchestrator that accepts a user query, parses it into subtasks, delegates to all workers concurrently, and merges the results.
...
class DelegationOrchestrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.agent = Agent(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")  # Replace with env var in production
        self.workers = {
            "search": search_worker,
            "calc": calc_worker,
            "summarize": summarize_worker
        }

    async def delegate(self, user_query: str) -> dict:
        """
        Parse query, spin off subagents in parallel, collect results.
        Returns a dict with {'search': ..., 'calc': ..., 'summarize': ...}
        """
        # 1. Determine which subagents are needed (keyword detection)
        needs_search = any(kw in user_query.lower() for kw in ["search", "find", "what"])
        needs_calc = any(kw in user_query.lower() for kw in ["calculate", "sum", "total"])
        has_math = any(ch in user_query for ch in ["+", "-", "*", "/", "="])

        # 2. Build tasks (Hermes Task wraps worker + input)
        tasks = {}
        if needs_search:
            tasks["search"] = Task(worker=search_worker, input=user_query)
        if needs_calc or has_math:
            tasks["calc"] = Task(worker=calc_worker, input=user_query)
        # Summarization always runs; trade-off: may use stale data if search is slower
        tasks["summarize"] = Task(worker=summarize_worker, input=user_query)

        # 3. Execute all tasks concurrently to reduce wall-clock time
        results = {}

        async def run_task(name, task):
            try:
                result = await self.agent.run_task(task)
                results[name] = result.output
                logger.info(f"{name} completed in {result.latency_ms:.1f}ms")
            except Exception as e:
                results[name] = f"ERROR: {str(e)}"
                logger.error(f"{name} failed: {e}")

        coros = [run_task(name, task) for name, task in tasks.items()]
        await asyncio.gather(*coros)

        return results
...
## Step 3: Production-Grade Error Handling and Monitoring
...
Sequential execution fails the moment one subagent times out. Hermes Agent 0.2.3 includes a circuit breaker and per-task`on_error` callbacks to prevent cascade failures.
...
class ProductionDelegationOrchestrator(DelegationOrchestrator):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        # threshold=3: open circuit after 3 consecutive failures
        # recovery_timeout=30: re-attempt after 30 seconds
        self.cb = CircuitBreaker(threshold=3, recovery_timeout=30)
        self.metrics = MetricsCollector(endpoint="http://metrics.internal:8000")

    async def delegate_with_retry(self, user_query: str) -> dict:
        start = time.perf_counter()
        try:
            # Skip orchestration if circuit breaker is open
            if self.cb.is_open():
                logger.warning("Circuit breaker open — returning cached response")
                return {"fallback": "Service temporarily unavailable"}

            results = await self.delegate(user_query)
            self.cb.record_success()

        except Exception as e:
            self.cb.record_failure()
            self.metrics.increment("delegation_failures", tags={"query": user_query[:20]})
            raise

        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
        self.metrics.timing("delegation_duration", elapsed)
        logger.info(f"Total delegation took {elapsed:.2f}s for {len(results)} subtasks")
        return results
...
In a load test with 10 parallel requests, delegation completed in under 1.2 seconds — approximately a 4.8x throughput improvement over sequential execution.
...
## How We Measured: Delegation Throughput and Latency
...
We benchmarked Hermes Agent v0.2.3 on Ubuntu 22.04 (Python 3.11,`gpt-4o-mini`) with the three-worker orchestrator from Step 2. Each test query triggered search + calc + summarize subagents on a mixed workload ("population + calculation" prompts).
...
- Throughput (tasks/s):`asyncio` load generator fired 10 parallel orchestration requests; measured completed delegations per second over a 30 s window. Sequential baseline awaited each subagent with`await` in series; delegation used`asyncio.gather`.
- Wall-clock p50 / p95 (ms): End-to-end time from`delegate()` call to merged result dict. Sequential sums subagent latencies; delegation is bounded by the slowest worker (search).
- Per-subagent latency: Parsed from Hermes Agent logs (`result.latency_ms`) over 50 warm runs.
...
Sequential processing handles 2.5 tasks/s. Delegation achieves 12.1 tasks/s — a 4.8× improvement with 10 parallel requests completing in under 1.2 s.
...
- Set per-worker timeouts — always lower than the overall request timeout
- Implement circuit breaker — prevent cascade failures across subagents
- Add retries with exponential backoff — handle transient API errors
- Instrument with metrics — track subagent latency, failure rate, and throughput
- Log all delegation decisions — include query hash and subagent selection
- Version worker prompts — store in config files to enable A/B testing
- Limit concurrency — use`asyncio.Semaphore` to avoid overwhelming downstream APIs
- Test with worst-case input — run chaos engineering to verify circuit breaker behavior
...
Hermes Agent subagent delegation is a pattern where a central orchestrator splits a complex query into smaller subtasks and routes each one to a specialized worker agent. Use it when your queries require more than one type of reasoning — for example, a question that needs web search and a calculation. Single-agent setups handle each subtask sequentially; delegation runs them in parallel, reducing total latency by up to 4.8x on three-worker configurations.
...
Yes — await each`Task` one at a time instead of using`asyncio.gather`. Sequential chaining is useful when a downstream worker depends on the output of an upstream one (for example, summarization that must wait for search results). The trade-off is higher total latency. For most production cases, use parallel execution with a final aggregator worker to merge results.
...
`CircuitBreaker(threshold=3, recovery_timeout=30)` opens the circuit after three consecutive failures. While open, all delegation calls return immediately with a fallback response, preventing downstream API overload. After 30 seconds, the circuit enters a half-open state and retries one request. If it succeeds,`record_success()` closes the circuit. If it fails again, the timeout resets. This avoids cascading failures when one subagent or external API becomes unavailable.

Hermes Agent is an open-source AI agent that lets you build your own self-hosted AI agent on a VPS, laptop, Docker container, or other machine. It can remember your preferences, use tools, run automations, and work through Telegram or other messaging apps.
...
Instead of only answering questions, Hermes can act. It can create files, run terminal commands, search past sessions, use skills, and schedule tasks with cron jobs.
...
In this guide, you’ll learn what Hermes Agent is, how it compares with OpenClaw and Claude Code, how to set it up safely, and which first workflows to build after setup.
...
Important fact: Hermes is built around memory, skills, soul, cron jobs, and self-improvement.
...
Practical takeaway: Start with one Telegram bot, one backup workflow, and one useful daily automation.

Hermes Agent is an open-source autonomous AI agent built by Nous Research. It lives on your server, remembers what it learns, and gets more capable the longer it runs, just like OpenClaw. So why it's different?
...
Self-improving skills loop. When it successfully solves a workflow, it turns that into a reusable skill → stored, searchable, and shareable.
...
Layered memory. It stores searchable session history and treats memory as a layered system of persistent notes, retrieval, and procedural knowledge.
...
Runs anywhere, talks anywhere. 6 terminal backends. You can talk to it from Telegram on your phone while it executes on a serverless worker.
...
But most people never get past the README. They see "self-hosted." They see 6 terminal options. They close the tab. Hermes Agent is not hard to use. It just has no beginner guide. Until now. In this guide, you will learn:
